text: From the Library of Congress and the Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture. Okay, okay. Good afternoon. Today is August 15, 2013. Speaking is David Klein from the History Department at Virginia Tech. Also working with the Southern oral history program at the University of North Carolina Chapel Hill. We are conducting an interview today for the Civil Rights History Project of the Library of Congress and the Smithsonian's National Museum of African American History and Culture. Behind the camera is John Bishop of Media Generation and we have the honor today of interviewing John and Jean Rosenberg. Jean is on Channel 1 and John is on Channel 2 and I just want to thank you both very much for being part of this project. Glad to be here. And we'll start today with we're going to start with Jean and I just wanted to... I usually start with people's family backgrounds in the belief that maybe you'll prove me wrong but that often the way that people are raised and the ideals and the influence, you can see later what a person ends up doing and so I'm curious, I'm always curious about the family life of people as they are raised. So if you could tell us a little bit about your backgrounds and whether you see any of those kind of... Oh, absolutely. I mean I think that's absolutely true in my observation of life too. So I was born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania and adopted by a transplanted New York couple and my father having been uprooted from his Presbyterian community was looking for a place to a spiritual growth and connected with the Quakers. And subsequently we... he joined and I joined and then later my brother and my mother joined the Quaker meeting in Abington, Pennsylvania. And that led to intense exposure to social action and commitment to the idea that there's that of God in every person. And that and also to the fact that people can arrive at decision making by consensus. And I think that that's a very important element in my later years that was a very good background for me to be living here in Appalachia. So I went from there, I went... Were there particular projects or focus, social justice focus in that meeting or...? Oh well of that meeting obviously that meeting and the Philadelphia yearly meeting were always involved in racial issues. And definitely in war concern about peace, the peace process and how to accomplish that. So I was always involved in those... that young friends movement as growing up and it guided me to apply to Earlham College in Richmond, Indiana, which is a Quaker school. And then I graduated there in June 1965. And you were born in 1943? Well you worked in... you went to a summer work camp in Mexico. Right, sure. But yeah. That sort of thing and your mom was on the picket line for peace, right? Right. That's what... Witness for peace, good old women. So let's hear a little bit. I mean just a part and parcel of my growing up years. And I in my high school at Abington High School, which is a suburban Philadelphia High School, refused to take part in ARA drills, as I felt that it was a psychology of fear and terror and that in fact in a truth hiding under your desk was not going to help you in a cold war situation. And therefore I refused. And so I was taken to the principal's office and called a Communist Dup. And then I was directed that when we had the drill I was to sit in front of the plate glass window so I'd be the first to be killed. So you know, when people do that, when they take those kinds of positions, then you are... You know, you're even more committed to thinking about what it is you're doing and why. And so I was able to arrange for a film called Operation Correction to go along. This is going on and on. But you know, I mean I arranged for that to be shown in my high school just so that people could see that there was another side to a story. And I felt that was very important. This is the second day in a row that Operation Correction has been mentioned. Really? Yeah. Where was he? He was a minister that showed her that his community got run out of town for a short while. Well, you know, luckily I was an honor student. So, you know, I think I got more leeway because I was able to do well on tests. So, and you started college in 1961? At Wilmington College. I did not go to Irland, initially. I went and I waited too long to apply and so I had the offer of waiting out a year and coming with an ex-class or... And I went to Wilmington College which is in Wilmington, Ohio, which is another Quaker School. And then I transferred in my junior year. So, and having been raised in the family and in the community that you've been telling us about, with a lot of awareness of issues, social issues and justice issues, how were you of other things that were happening in the country? I was part of the civil rights movement, but were you aware of earlier traditions and what was going on? You know, were there particular movements for you? Well, I mean, I was very aware of the Vietnam War and the lead up to that. And I was one of the protesters at my at Irland, you know, that would go regularly to stand and witness against the war. And it was, however, my friend Dorothy, we referred to as Landsberg. She was Dorothy Shelton, then. I met her at Irland College and she was involved with... Her father was an internal revenue lawyer in Washington, D.C. And he helped get her a summer internship with John Dorr in the summer of 64, where she worked very hard on the developing an index of... from the FBI reports on clan members, which was then subsequently helpful when jury selection took place in a number of cases. And, well, I mean, we were all... she was part of the student at, you know, political activities committee and so was I. And then I met John Dorr. That's when I met John Dorr. She invited him to speak at a convocation at Irland. And she didn't want to introduce him because she was afraid he would tease her in front of everyone. So she got her buddy Jean to introduce him. And while we were eating together, he said, well, have you ever thought about joining me establishment? And I said, no. And he said, why don't you do that? And why don't you give it a try? And so since I wasn't focused on where I would be after I graduated, we had thought about going to law school, but that wasn't really a passion. So I did, in fact, apply and was accepted as a research analyst for the Civil Rights Division, the Department of Justice in June of 65. And I then went on to qualify my loyalty oath and I still had the job. And what did you say? I've inserted in the loyalty oath that I would, in so far as my conscience would allow, I would, you know, support and defend the United States. So they kept me anyway. And that was when I started working as a research analyst. And went from, we worked on all kinds of cases. We worked on school desegregation cases, police brutality cases, employment cases, during the time that I was there. I left in the spring of 1970. Okay. So let's switch over to John and then we'll bring him up to that same moment. So John, if you could introduce yourself and a little bit about your background as well, it would be wonderful. Well, I'm John Rosenberg. I'm Jean's husband of 46 years. She's put up with me over that period, hopefully for another maybe not 46, but a few more. I was born in Germany October 7, 1931, a long time ago. My father was a teacher in the Jewish school system. I say the Jewish school system. He actually, when Adolf Hitler set up a secret required the schools to be segregated so that Jewish kids were separated out. He and another man set up the school for Jewish kids and the city was named Magdeburg, Germany. The city then, about 300,000 people, a large industrial center, which was bombed pretty heavily during the war. And I grew up there and then as a young child, the first day of school is a big deal. And if I remember, you go to school, start around Easter and they give you a big conical bag with all kinds of gifts in it. I say that because we have, there's a family picture of me holding this conical bag with our gifts on the first day of school. But I was actually, by that point, my father's class. He was a teacher and then I also worked for the Jewish welfare agency. He was teaching courses to people who were interested in going to Israel. He wanted to go to Israel, but my mother said, no, we are, if we leave the country, we're going to go to the United States because her mother and father and two sisters had immigrated to the country and she wasn't really interested in going to Israel. Although he had had one sister and a brother go to Israel at an early time as into a small cooperative community. In any event, we lived in a building next to a synagogue. We had an apartment there because dad worked for that welfare agency and he also assisted the rabbi and the local congregation. We were there during Kristallnacht and they bombed the synagogue. They didn't, they were somewhat careful about the synagogue because there was a hospital next door. Otherwise, as you probably know, all the other synagogues, primarily were burned down or the ones most synagogues in Germany were burned. The way my dad was in a concentration camp for 11 days and we were fortunate enough to get out of the country. We came, we spent a year in Holland. My father started a school in this detention camp and we had no materials but he taught by the Socratic Method teaching us verbally. We came to this country on February 22, 1940 on pretty much the last ship. His brother and mother were on board. I'm not sure about his mother. I think both of another ship of several weeks lighter in the ship was required. It was turned around because the war started. They didn't get out. His brother was killed. He lost the, there was another family. One of his sisters had married. The fellow was on a stock exchange who was, they were fairly well off. They had five kids in Rotterdam and that family was gone and family in Germany. Most of his, so he lost his substantial and his mother was also killed during that period. She lived right very close to the end of the war. In fact, his brother were married during the, while he was in the concentration camp and we met his widow in Israel several years later. But so we came and then we, my father when we came to this country, it was very difficult to find a job. There were so many immigrants in New York. And so he learned of community in Spartanburg, South Carolina and Gastonian North Carolina. They had substantial Jewish communities but no rabbi. And so he, that's what drew him there. I mean, we always, I'm not sure which comes first. But during the week he swept floors in a textile mill and on weekends he alternated going to these two communities. And I always thought it was pretty amazing because he was writing sermons when he could hardly just still learning to speak English. But after three years we then moved. He learned of a better position in a textile mill in Gastonia or in Lowell North Carolina. Fellow who came from our hometown who in Magdeburg, a parent who had it mill they are brought a bunch of equipment and started textile mill. And the idea learned went to work as an office manager if I remember right. But he basically learned the textile business and stayed as a white collar worker and was very active in the local synagogue and I grew up in town of Gastonia for practical purposes. We'd been in Spartanburg for three years. So I grew up in Gastonia and you know worked after school and scouting was a big part of my life. I was an eagle scout and spent summers as a counselor and went off to Duke University as a freshman. May I ask what the racial dynamics were in Gastonia at that time? I would first tell about the fact that being a German immigrant they took away the binoculars. I just think that's an interesting caveat. After the war started when we were aliens, my parents weren't citizens and Gene always said the FBI or Treasury agents came to our apartment one day knocked on the door and they required they took the binoculars that my parents had and the shortwave, the radio that had a capability of getting shortwave. But they were quite embarrassed to see that we had Franklin Delano Roosevelt's picture on the wall and you have this fairly patriotic thankful family to be in this country and they came and took this equipment away from us. It was I think mutually embarrassed very. They eventually brought it back after the war. We got this stuff back. But we were Gastonia was a segregated society. We had black schools. We had you were living in a segregated society. You always think back on those days the Jewish community, most of the members of that community as was true and many southern communities were pretty well off. They had their parents and grandparents had come to these communities or come to the area of his petalers and started selling merchandise and then eventually built a retail store. The community was well off. I'm not sure that anybody had ever seen a portrait of what we were relatively poor Jewish working family until we got there. It was a nice community and several other people were. I think they respected my father a lot because he had such a huge knowledge of Jewish culture and could read Hebrew. We could quote the next verse in the Torah for five books of Moses if you started him anywhere in there which was not that unusual for someone who had all that biblical background or studied in the some he had been to the seminary. He actually came from family with nine children in northwest Germany and one of his early teaching assignments was in my mother's hometown. She was a young 16 year old and they got to know each other and by when she was 18 they got married. He was working in the mill and I had some of jobs in the mill and it was as you lived as you know, every Jewish community was thankful to be there economically. They couldn't get into the country club for many despite their wealth. Most of them were not at that point. The country clubs were segregated. But they certainly took no major initiatives in trying to help integrate the community or call attention to the fact that this is wrong. I think you basically grew up with it and when you move into that system looking back, I'm sure my parents were so grateful to be there that they were. That was the last thing they had in mind. Although my parents, my mother especially, I mean my summer job, I worked off when you're in the mill you interact with lots of black folks. But everybody kind of knows where the lines are. I don't know if there was any Gastonia's history is sort of interesting. There are book called preachers and mill hands. They had riots in the 20s and 30s around the textile worker issue and working conditions. But it wasn't racial. It wasn't that was not a racial issue. So the schools were totally segregated and that's just the way life was. My mother had my mom was known to be a very friendly person. She was and didn't start work. She decided after the kids were all gone that she wanted to be a saleswoman to do work in the local department store which she did. But she had people coming by where they were black or white and she would interact with them and offer them bite the eat or whatever. And I think the family in that sense, I didn't have don't recall. I don't think I had any friends in the black community. A few years ago, not too many years ago, 10 years ago I think Gastonia, the class president of the year before me or two years, promoted a joint anniversary of the classes from 1948 so that the black and white graduates would now be adults would be together. I think it was a one time thing. But I remember that Gaston County Gaston County is actually one of the voting rights cases. I went to the Supreme Court. I think they challenged the mechanism under which Gaston County was selected. But from that I was a Duke in a, I went to undergraduate school at Duke. It wasn't, it was sort of between Duke and the University of North Carolina. And there was a trustee at the time named C Park who was from Gastonia. And he encouraged me to go to Duke because he thought he could secure and he did secure a tuition scholarship for me for the first four, what we hope would be four years. Unfortunately, he died after the first year. And I was then interviewed and said, you're making too much money working in the dining room where you got chips for food or whatever. And they said, you got to have to pay your own tuition. Which I did and stayed in, but it was always sort of gosh. I was pretty poor. My dad was sending me 25 bucks a month or something and they took away the tuition scholarship. But I did get, I was a chem major Duke and I in ROTC. And so I ended, we went into the United States Air Force. And I spent three years in England as an navigator. London, very close to a lot of my colleagues had a very interesting year fly. We went, at that time it was a period of the Cold War. So many of our exercises were on the close to the Russian border. We dropped the special forces and a very military period. Totally contrary to what I was picketing the Pentagon. Right. Well, she was picketing the Pentagon. So after I came out of the service, I worked for a year for a chemical firm, Roman Hassan, Philadelphia. And I was just asking about the air force. The air force was integrated at that point. What was your experience there in terms of? Well, I think it was integrated. And it was sort of in one of the things I relate as there was pretty much a life changing experience for me. Because we were very close to each other, black sand whites. And we, on one of those occasions, we brought a plane back to this country. And the radar operator was a fellow from Charleston, South Carolina named Abe Jenkins. And when we brought the plane back to Long Island, we went by way of Iceland and then Greenland. It was a little, it was an SA-16 called a Grumman Albatross, which flies at about 160 miles an hour. So if you've got a very heavy headwind, you might be moving along at 20 or 30 miles an hour over the ground, which on at least one occasion we did. Because of the island, we were just about to run out of fuel anyway. We got back when we got to Long Island, even I had a week to go home. And we got on the train together in New York. And we got to Washington. We were in uniform. And when we got to Washington, Abe said he would see me later. And I said, where are you going? He said, I'm going to the back of the train where the blacks are. And I said, and he did. And I thought, this is bad. This is really wrong. And maybe I'm sure in the back of my mind, maybe somewhere along the way, I'll have a chance to do something about that, which show. So you're right. It was a, I mean, in the sense that I ended up going to silver. I'm sure that particular incident had a lot to do with the way, I mean, it sorry, hit me in the head. I don't know whether it was the first trip back when we were together. I went to an navigation school with many black members and it never seemed to be. I'm going to cut just for a minute. Yeah. That mosquito. That mosquito is coming from me. I'm going to take little breaks. It was interesting because in navigation school, my four of us lived together off base, four students and two of them from Texas and one was from New Orleans. And it was at a time when people were we, I think, I don't think I had, I didn't have a car, but I know that one of them did and we had this Confederate Air Force sticker on there. And it was still the institution was as an institution, but there was still a fair amount of racism and joking and that sort of thing. And going on and it was all part of the way you lived, but you also, I think, I think they saw that the members, the black officers who were in navigation training at that time, you know, it was their first, everybody's first experience in a major way of living together, working together and eventually having to depend on each other. So that was just leading up to the time that we were when I went to England. And then we were, you have crew members and you're in some fairly dangerous situations and you just know for sure that the color of your skin didn't make any difference at all. You're involved in a very difficult exercise in which they're depending on you or you're depending on them as much as you are any other anybody else. And for you personally, I don't know whether if there are African Americans in your classes at Duke or was this really for you the first time to be in Coast Park Simulator? It was. Duke was still a white school. Duke was still segregated. I had a Jewish quota. Yeah, they had a quota for Jewish, which I don't think I was aware of in the sense that, you know, we thought, well, this is the number. But when I went to Duke, the two roommates I had were both Jewish. We weren't put there by accident. But there was a Jewish fraternity. Did you come into each other about that? I don't know. You know, it turned out that one of them and I were lifelong friends. The other one of them became a doctor. Norman Rosenbaum went to the University of Virginia and ended up in New England. And he was always, Norman was much more of a loner. He played, he was from Norfolk and had played basketball. But he always went off to study on his own. And then Marty and I ultimately joined the Jewish fraternity, which was a big, new experience for me. I've never been with a lot of Jews. I was president of my senior class in high school. And just one of the boys, as it were, you know, you felt you were a member of, I don't know, that the Jewishness, I never felt any real, religious discrimination. I mean, we were always, as Gene said, she was a very good student. People, I had a younger brother who did, who was also doing well in school. And I was involved in a lot of activities and always working after school. And, you know, so there was people I think did respect the family. And as I say, I was president of my sophomore class and then I gave my senior class. So I lost my career thought. I wanted to say about my history is interestingly enough when I went to Wilmington College, which is a Quaker School, they intentionally assigned me a black roommate because they didn't know how the other gals on my hall would respond to being integrated in 61. So I got the black roommate from the Bronx and we've been lifelong friends. But it's interesting, you know, I mean, because I was a Quaker. You know, it wasn't, you know, just because you go to a Quaker School there few and far between usually. And she has been well. And so the three of us, Marty, this other third, the two roommates, Marty, Sak and I, he recently passed away. He was a lawyer in Jacksonville. And he was, and we became very, very close. As I say, we both joined the fraternity and the third roommate didn't. He was, instead independent on pretty much on his own and so Marty and I, but you had this Jewish fraternity on campus at Duke, which at that time was only Jewish. And after my second year, I was a delegate to a national convention in which the charter was changed to allow non-Jewish members, although it's still primarily a Jewish fraternity, I think. But it did expose me to a whole new system. I mean, I, this, it was very helpful to, it was nice. These were pretty close. There were very few Jewish women on the, these campus, but they were also integrated. So there was a conscious effort by the administration at that time to keep the number of students down. Of course, today it's, there's, there's multi-national, multi-cultural, multi-religious, a very, very fine institution. But I then went to the, after I worked for the chemical company for a year, I was thinking I wanted to do something that might be a little more useful and maybe use my brain a little bit and decide to go to the, to law school. And then whether I really figured out already that I was going to be on the civil rights division, I'm not sure. I, I, Jewish had a fairly small, he was a very Jewish chambers who then was number one in our became number one in our class had a fairly small circle of non of white friends socially or to the extent that he was going to spend any time. And he spent any time not studying. So we did become friendly during those, those years at Chapel Hill and kind of kept up with each other over the years. I was kept up sort of with his great, his great career. And he would, we would exchange letters. And once we'll always see each other at, you know, at conferences and he'd send a little money to Apple Red when I was over here. So from the, so after I finished law school, I was pretty determined to be in the civil rights division. And that's, I actually spent the first month and a half in the annual trust division waiting to get an interview with John Doar, who at that time was going down to the University of Mississippi every week too, because it was the time of getting James Meredith into oldness into the University of Mississippi. And so after I interviewed with John, there were only eight or nine lawyers at that time in the division. Then I was delighted to start. So why civil rights? And you said early on in your locker room, you saw an interest there. What was it? Well, I mean, I think it was a combination of having been born in Germany and seeing and thinking about the hall of this Holocaust history of ours and the terrible genocide that went subject to the history of the history of the whole world. And then seeing that the Jewish people were, throughout Europe were subjected to. And then seeing really in having been in the service with people who were black and thinking that this is a caste system is wrong in this country, being very proud of this country. And I'm still, I think it's the greatest place to find this country in the world. And there's just some things that we need to keep working on to make it better. And I feel I felt that this was a place I could make a contribution. I thought that maybe as a southerner to some extent communication wise, it's a little easier. I don't know that I'd hoped to change any of how many people I've changed whose mind I would change. But it just seemed that the division, which was representing the United States in these cases, and that we would, that you have the force of the country behind you and that it was going to be difficult work and challenging work that was just kind of getting underway. The division was created just 1957. And the, really didn't have very much authority to do very much itself. And the lawyers who had been working sort of as civil rights division lawyers have been sitting at their desks in Washington and were kind of reviewing papers and didn't do it very much until John Doerr came in 1960 with six months to go and Eisenhower administration and realize what you've got to do is go to the south and talk to the people who are having the problems. And try to do something. And that was he being a trial lawyer. He said you got to go find out what the facts are. And so he was working on those, he was quite a heroic. I thought for him to take this job was six months to go. He got a call and from the eye, nobody else was willing to do it. And so he was actually working on one of these cases in Louisiana where a cotton farmer had registered to vote and the cotton ginners then conspired to refuse to gin as cotton because he's got name was Charles Atlas the same as the body builder. And so the story goes if Bobby Kennedy would become attorney general was reviewing the pleading in the affidavits in those cases and saw and had talked with John and realized that this was really a very special person who could get some things done. I think there is a little more to it. He actually had a conversation about where things were and John explained to him by that time during after he was sort of in charge during the transition from one administration to the next. And so Bobby Kennedy asked John to stay on and then he did and eventually became also assistant attorney general but he was in charge of the trial work and he taught us basically his system of trying cases. So anyway, we can talk if you want to go back to Gene or how would you want to go back and forth. I just want to follow up on that. How closely did the attorney general and the division work? Oh, very much so. I mean, I think the attorney general Bobby Kennedy at that point was the boss and he he but he clearly wanted us to when I think the mission of the division and at that time in 1962 was really from a legal standpoint to challenge the voting system of voting discrimination against blacks throughout the South. Now, other things happened and that was like James Meredith couldn't anticipate the timing of when he was going to go to Ole Miss in 1962. And so protecting him became an issue that took considerable division time. Lawyers were spending the or my I don't know, I gave his name Gerald Stern who is another lawyer who's in Washington who actually tried one of the big. Colmining cases here a few years ago and wrote a book called the Buffalo Creek disaster. Gerald while I was waiting to see John, John would initially spend all his time with Meredith. I have a really nice picture in him and I can get a McChain Marshall McChain and John with James Meredith being confronted by Lieutenant Governor Johnson when they weren't going to admit him. We might want to put in here. No, it's not when here it's up on my wall and the other room. I have to bring it down. But after reconstruction, blacks were basically prohibited from voting. I mean they wiped the rolls clean in most of these counties. In these rural counties, Mississippi and Alabama, I was assigned. Initially I spent a short amount of time in Georgia. There had been a series of church burnings and I mean this efforts to protest, the failure to let people register to vote were going on all over in these various states. We were assigned geographically and the first few months I was in Georgia working with a lawyer named Jerry Hyalbrun who had practiced in Fort Smith Arkansas for many years was one of the few private attorneys who actually, few lawyers who were private attorneys but who, when the racial issues started coming up, was viewed as a liberal and then basically lost all his business and Burke Marshall heard about it. They connected and he came up and spent some time eventually went to the community relations service. Jerry was sort of a glad handler but a very liberal and is thinking and wanted to do something about racial discrimination. So I worked with Jerry for a little while and then we actually one of our very first cases with not a very good outcome. Deputy Sheriff in Sassar, Georgia, which is near America and that very difficult area, you know the area. There were three or four black students who had gone from door to door to register people to vote and he chased them out of town with a pistol. And so we filed a criminal information and we had a trial in federal court and we need to move stuff. Anyway, the jury wasn't out very long but it was my first case to put on some witnesses and... How difficult was to get with it? And I'm... Van Hoffman. Van Hoffman, post. Yes, I remember. I was always dodging him. Okay. No, I'm not going to tell you that. Well, okay. So... Oh, you were asking, we were talking about the... This was my first experience in a federal criminal prosecution with the Deputy Sheriff. The jury wasn't out terribly long. That's right. I was just about to ask how difficult it was to find people willing to testify. In this case, the major witnesses were these black students who had been affected. And interesting sidelight, although I always thought a lot of Jerry Hyalburn was that he had... he was late getting there. I think a plane was late. So the judge wanted to start the case. So I actually examined the witnesses. And the early witnesses, before we got to the cross-examination of the deputy, which was after the jury was going to do it. But I was calling these black students by their last names, Mr. Mrs. Mrs. Mrs. So I could see from looking around, this was people who thought that was pretty unusual. And I'm not sure that even Jerry agreed with it a little later. I don't recall later whether he in his summation referred to them by their first names. He was still of the view, I think, that we could persuade southerners to comply, because even coming from his community in Fort Smith, I think he felt bad about leaving it, you know, you kind of... That so he was still trying to sort of... well, if we're going to be a little more southerner about the way we... if we're more... if they'll understand us, maybe we can make things happen in a consensual way, as the Quakers would say. I think the clan wasn't about to do that, and many others weren't about to do it. But then I was transferred over to Mississippi towards the end of 1962 in the beginning of 1963. But the answer to your question is, I think... well, it was in that case, the three black students were willing to testify. I think it was always took a lot of courage. Were they local? Were they coming up? They were local. These three students that had been chased away, I think, were from another community, because he chased them out of town. I can't remember the details of it, but they were... I think all three of them were black, and I think they'd been recruited by SNCC or one of the groups from Albany. They may have been part of the Albany. There was a large movement in Albany, and I went to Albany several times during... I'm just vague on that memory, because I spent a little time there, but CB King was administered... I had spoken... had gone to and met with several times, and trying to... What we were trying to do was be prepared and to get information back to the Attorney General's office or the Civil Rights Division about what was happening in these communities. We didn't have any success. Really, I'm in the Georgia Church burnings. I think those were among the first things that were events, and they were at night. Those witnesses that you would have had to have some way into the white community or into the clan or whoever did those shootings and burnings. We got better at it, I think, as time went on. The FBI got better with it as time went on after the Civil Rights workers were murdered, especially. Then I moved over to the Mississippi section. How did monitoring work? Blacks should have been allowed to register to vote, but it often had problems. How were those things monitored? But I think the best thing that happened was that the voters threw the school board out, and they threw out this lawyer on the other side, and that had happened in many places. This lawyer became a millionaire off the school case. I mean, he was a corporate lawyer, but they'd been resisting desegri in his school system for years, and this lawyer had made a living off that really for many years. And the same thing in Little Rock, I had a couple of cases in Arkansas. This big firm in downtown Little Rock, representing these school districts, these rural school districts that were still totally segregated. And they were, I mean, they did other work, but they were well paid to keep the system segregated. And so in Houston, they finally ditched him, and they had a whole new school board. And I think that over time, Houston, I mean, it's an enormous system. Now it's twice as big as it was when we were there. But I think they've really reformed them on their schools. But those are different. Same thing with employment cases. I mean, we haven't mentioned them. When we started when I became chief of the Western section, we sued the electrical workers in Las Vegas, because the union was, blacks couldn't get a job. You couldn't get into the apprenticeship, couldn't get into the union because you were black, which you hardly ever realized. And it's still pretty much a segregated town. I mean, the housing patterns, we sued the steam fitters in Los Angeles, sued the roadway express. That was James Case, roadway trucking company with my, well, they were in Birmingham, right? Yeah, they were in Birmingham. Yeah, but the division, the same thing with schools afterwards. There were a lot of schools in Indianapolis, schools in Oklahoma City as the, as the after the Civil Rights Act of 64, the division really expanded its work into other parts of the country as well. I mean, it was not on, it needed to happen because the all segregation didn't just happen in the South. It might have been more de facto in some areas in the larger, much more difficult to do the Chicago school case or Indianapolis. So let's use this as a segue then into your move out of out of the division and to hear and the work that you took on here and this idea of taking on other social justice issues and how these maybe related one to the other. Well, I mean, I think they are, as they are related. I mean, we hoped we could, I guess I was hoping that I could continue doing public interest work. I did talk to some private firms, but we went on this big camping trip and wasn't quite sure. But we left the division because the administration changed and the cases that got filed, what congressional district they were in seemed to become more important than what was in the case. Well, and they retreated on school, these are the issues. I mean, I think it not an easy decision and there was some civil rights division lawyer revolts. I don't know if you remember, there was some of that history when they felt that the division wasn't doing enforcing the law the way they should. And actually that took most of the half of my way were in Houston. So, but whether it belonged to, if you are going to leave, especially if you're in a position of some responsibility, whether they're going to fill your shoes with somebody who is not going to do the kind of work at the level that you were doing it. People who are really committed should you stay and fight it out and try to push him to do that or can you. But we decided also because I think if you stay in government service, you sort of start pricing yourself out of the mark. I mean, you're making fairly good money. You're doing fairly good economically too. And if you're going to switch, of course we took a big die, you know, we took our money. It's only half. Well, we came here, but we do, you know, we decided money wasn't all that important to us. Were you tired too? Did you need a break from that taste or not? Well, we went on this camping trip for three months with a little baby. Went to Canada and went to all the national parks. I mean, we sort of left in the beginning of the summer and decided then we formally didn't leave, I think, when we came back. But we went on a put up a tent or learned how to put up a tent in New Jersey, which we bought at a Morse end store. And then we went up to the state, up the coast, up the New End, up to Canada. We took probably a couple of months and our Michael was what, three, four, he was six months old. Jean was breastfeeding. And we just got away from it. And right after we left one of the firms, one we come back for an interview. And I said, no, we didn't want to do that. But along the way, one of our colleagues, Terry Lensner, who is, a book is going to come out, who also was at that point, the director of legal services for the Office of Economic Opportunity for OEO. And they had started some legal services programs with OEO money. There wasn't a lot of that going. Most of those programs were in the Northeast where, at that time, under the Office of Economic Opportunity Act, you could only get a project if the governor agreed. You had to stay, you had to stay, you had to stay okay for whatever happened. And so there were a few legal service programs outside of the Northeast, California. So this, Terry had been approached by some lawyers in Charleston who once started public interest law and was public interest law firm focused on the coal issues, symptomatic issues of poverty and the coal fields. And so he got in touch with us and said, why don't you go down and take a look at this. I would like, I'm willing to put a little money in starting supporting this kind of work in Appalachia. And so we came down, we stopped and visited with folks in Charleston, and then we came down here, camped out here at General Iowa State Park, put the tent up. Jean said you better see if there any bears around. Foxes, they're wanting somebody out there but us. I mean, it was at the end of the season. I figured if there were animals that were eating off the campers and there were no more campers. So we did, we were actually on our way to Florida. It was high holidays. Jewish high where we were going to go. To my parents who had retired to Florida. So we came down here and we interviewed Harry, Harry Coddle and White Spurg who wrote, night comes to the Comberlands and was on. He was on the Apple Red Board and was Virginia that already started a little corporation up there. And just for the record Apple Red stands for Apple Action Research and Defense Fund. That was the original name and several years later when we had to split the programs because of political problems. I mean, we weren't welcomed down here anyway. So we came here and we, the Preston's Burg, we came to Preston's Burg. We came to Charleston and talked to the folks who were there that Terry had talked with. And then there were, there was some lawyers who were unlicensed who were already here and then fell on him to Howard Thorkelson. And Howard was going to leave and they asked us to, he was going to be the deputy for Westford for Kentucky. Anyway, so he was leaving. So we came to Preston's Burg and then drove down to White Spurg to talk to Coddle and said, we'd think about it and drove and drove on down to see my parents and the real thought was there, there are some real environmental issues, there are some poverty issues. There are certainly a lot of poverty and there are very few lawyers who are going to, who appear to be available to challenge these. Most of these private attorneys were tied up by co-companies and the issues that were described to us by Harry, this broad formed deed and the environmental issues around strip mining and coal mine health and safety, Black Long. And the poverty issues consumer law and there were a lot of, at that time there were a lot of basic issues around food stamps and Medicaid and the early parts of that program applicants were given a very, had a difficult time. Local time, just local welfare offices were very, very negative and were not very, they weren't working with the NIGG ARDO. Anyway, it was just, how could your dad spelling? Oh no. They were just terrible, I mean, they, they, they, you would think they were treating these applicants so badly and people were having a difficult time getting food stamps. Very, very much the same. Food family were. Who you got and jobs were all very political because the coal that only two industry, the largest employer invariably in these counties was the co-companies and the second largest were the school districts. And they were very politicized and there was much of a fiefdom as we saw in the white power structure in the, in the south. But I think, you know, what, what was your area that you covered? Well, we started, I mean, technically the organization was for the Appalachian counties in Kentucky, of which there were 42. We subsequently gave, gave away, we gave moved six counties into another legal services programs, the ones around Ashland. But the program covered these 37 counties and so when, when I first came, we had one or two, we had an office on the campus University of Kentucky when in Barbaraville and here. We eventually, when the Legal Services Corporation Act was passed and finally there was federal funding. There was a lot of controversy about the legislation to create a structure for free legal services programs, a lot of opposition. When we came to here, there was a lot of opposition from the local bar because they thought we were taking money away from them. And the community because they thought we were communist? Well, that too. Because our predecessors, they had a group of, a group of, Apple, call of activist outside agitators, call, they were Appalachian volunteers who had come here to volunteer in various poverty programs. And they too lived with local families and encouraged them to apply for benefits and health care. And hospitals at that time were turning away people didn't have any money. They just told me we don't do it. Unwent, I mean pregnant moms couldn't get a delivery. A lot of terrible stories. The situation was really pretty grim. So, and these... A little while to work your way into the... Well, I mean, there was a case of when Supreme Court involving the Appalachian volunteers, where I think one of the volunteers was... Volunteers had threatened a local official or might have even slugged him. I can't map them. No, he slugged them. Well, I don't know. Clark, did he hit them? I can't say that. There was a lot of controversy and they... And that, there was outside agitators and spades. And they were trying to encourage people to apply for benefits and to get... And that sort of thing. And there was... Health care was very... It was very poor. I mean, there were one of our friends started eventually, started a free clinic out here on Mud Creek. But there were all those issues that... If you're trying to promote social justice or environmental justice, but... And make the system operate a little more fairly, so that poor people have a chance. And then it isn't just who you are. And that you have... So often, at that level, a lawyer makes a big difference. Many of these people have legal issues. Family problems. Disability problems. I mean, disability benefits. Consumer law, you can't... How can you deal with it for closure if you don't have a lawyer? So your play, I'm sure, in the 43 years has been full all the time. I mean, especially covering such a wide area. Absolutely. Do you have a full play? You've been there. So when we were 10 off, we expanded to 10 off... 11 offices. Okay. And this was our main administrative office. And then we started back when I retired in 2002. We said we were fortunate we started a separate nonprofit to do primarily focus on the coal issues. Call the Appalachian Citizens Law Center. But over that time, when we expanded, each of these offices would cover several counties. We were hoping we would have four lawyers, at least in every office. So we could do the day-to-day work and do a major, a larger case. We sued the state... Like we sued the state park system. Because they were discriminating against blacks. Still, couldn't get a job in the state park system. If you were black, primarily you'd end up at the bottom. That was familiar. But that takes a fair amount of resources. And locally and a local, a little office. We did a lot of those fairly major cases. And day-to-day divorces for people are custody matters that all of them take time. And no private attorney is going to do them if they're not going to pay you. So we have... What I hope to do is have a first-class law firm for poor people away. John Doerr wanted to put a good bunch of lawyers together for... to represent the government. So I think we did some of that. I think we had a wonderful terrific group of lawyers who were in fairly remote locations in small towns in eastern Kentucky. So you saw that as something you could do to train that next generation, the next cadre? I hope there was a good goal. I think that's what I wanted to do. And hopefully these folks when you are in the community. One of the big differences I do think is that I learned to appreciate was that we were in the south and spent a lot of time preparing cases, but we went home to Washington. When you're here and folks are in their communities, they also become part of the local community. So if our kids are in these schools, we care about these schools. And so Jean saw in a statewide advocacy school. Whether we've been working on education for many years, not just us, but what you want. You're trying to create a better community for everybody. And whether it's schools or the environment. And the environment is hard because coal miners want... That's their jobs. And when people start complaining that regulations are preventing them from mining coal, it makes it hard to try to demonstrate that you need regulations to protect the environment. And that that can be done. You can mine coal responsibly and comply with regulations at the same time. I mean, you have to... If you're going to care about these mountains, not a lot of these companies or out-of-state companies. Don't care about them. For many years, all the coal underground was owned primarily by out-of-state interest, and they weren't paying any taxes on that. So we were able to get that changed. It's a strange thing about extraction industries. Oh, yeah. So oil is a pretty special allowance, for example. Will that be taken as much as you can? Then we'll pay you a bonus for all the money you've lost by having taken it out? Get a little extra. Well, unfortunately, Kentucky, Alaska and some of the other states had at least set up some trust funds and put away some money for later on when the severance taxes... They've never had the... We have a severance tax here, but most of the lot of that money was spent on roads and economic development that didn't really pan out. So this is a very difficult period here. It's not quite what we're talking about, which is there isn't coal miners are losing their jobs. And no one has over time been able to demonstrate how to come up with other industries or other replacements. I mean, we're at a global competition. We've done things like get our little science museum started. Right here, we have a nice planetarium. I saw the science from that. Oh, yeah. We saw the science coming in. It took about 10 years. We have a very... I mean, the history of it is really the growing up in Gastonia where we now have a wonderful natural history museum. That was the father, which was a scout executive I was with. But so we started talking about trying to do outreach to math and science in math and science in this region in our county because our scores were so low that students were doing badly. So badly. And I was hoping that we could get that... If we get some brain power going early in math and science, we might be able to affect the future. Maybe we have some smart kid who will figure out how to do a Silicon Hall or instead of a Silicon Valley. So we built a very... We were able... Because the governor was from the next county and the speaker was from this county. We politically over a couple of legislatures. We got enough money together to build this very nice facility with the planetarium. And a multipurpose classroom and some exhibit space and that sort of thing. A really nice resource for a community this size. But there are other things going on here. There's a re-grossing. And John was on the advisory board and I was on the advisory board for the local job course center. And we just want you to know they also had a rocky start here. But they were very helped by citizens involvement and stuff like that. Of course, me and I think it's... I'm just going to ask you if you would sing us down. Sing us out with a... We're done. We're concluding statement. Sing us down. What do you think? Well, we've had a very... When you reflect on your life, you feel very fortunate when your values are congruent with what you're doing day to day. And we've been able to do that. Can we thank you? Ta-da. Cha-ta-cha. This has been a presentation of the Library of Congress and the Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture.

segments.text: From the Library of Congress and the Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture.
Okay, okay. Good afternoon. Today is August 15, 2013. Speaking is David Klein from the History Department at Virginia Tech.
Also working with the Southern oral history program at the University of North Carolina Chapel Hill.
We are conducting an interview today for the Civil Rights History Project of the Library of Congress and the Smithsonian's National Museum of African American History and Culture.
Behind the camera is John Bishop of Media Generation and we have the honor today of interviewing John and Jean Rosenberg.
Jean is on Channel 1 and John is on Channel 2 and I just want to thank you both very much for being part of this project.
Glad to be here.
And we'll start today with we're going to start with Jean and I just wanted to...
I usually start with people's family backgrounds in the belief that maybe you'll prove me wrong but that often the way that people are raised and the ideals and the influence,
you can see later what a person ends up doing and so I'm curious, I'm always curious about the family life of people as they are raised.
So if you could tell us a little bit about your backgrounds and whether you see any of those kind of...
Oh, absolutely. I mean I think that's absolutely true in my observation of life too.

So I was born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania and adopted by a transplanted New York couple and my father having been uprooted from his Presbyterian community was looking for a place to a spiritual growth and connected with the Quakers.
And subsequently we... he joined and I joined and then later my brother and my mother joined the Quaker meeting in Abington, Pennsylvania.
And that led to intense exposure to social action and commitment to the idea that there's that of God in every person.
And that and also to the fact that people can arrive at decision making by consensus.
And I think that that's a very important element in my later years that was a very good background for me to be living here in Appalachia.
So I went from there, I went...
Were there particular projects or focus, social justice focus in that meeting or...?
Oh well of that meeting obviously that meeting and the Philadelphia yearly meeting were always involved in racial issues.
And definitely in war concern about peace, the peace process and how to accomplish that.
So I was always involved in those... that young friends movement as growing up and it guided me to apply to Earlham College in Richmond, Indiana, which is a Quaker school.
And then I graduated there in June 1965.
And you were born in 1943?
Well you worked in... you went to a summer work camp in Mexico.
Right, sure. But yeah.
That sort of thing and your mom was on the picket line for peace, right?
Right.
That's what...
Witness for peace, good old women.
So let's hear a little bit.
I mean just a part and parcel of my growing up years.
And I in my high school at Abington High School, which is a suburban Philadelphia High School, refused to take part in ARA drills,
as I felt that it was a psychology of fear and terror and that in fact in a truth hiding under your desk was not going to help you in a cold war situation.
And therefore I refused. And so I was taken to the principal's office and called a Communist Dup.
And then I was directed that when we had the drill I was to sit in front of the plate glass window so I'd be the first to be killed.
So you know, when people do that, when they take those kinds of positions, then you are...
You know, you're even more committed to thinking about what it is you're doing and why.
And so I was able to arrange for a film called Operation Correction to go along.
This is going on and on.
But you know, I mean I arranged for that to be shown in my high school just so that people could see that there was another side to a story.
And I felt that was very important.
This is the second day in a row that Operation Correction has been mentioned.
Really?
Yeah. Where was he? He was a minister that showed her that his community got run out of town for a short while.
Well, you know, luckily I was an honor student.
So, you know, I think I got more leeway because I was able to do well on tests.
So, and you started college in 1961?
At Wilmington College. I did not go to Irland, initially.
I went and I waited too long to apply and so I had the offer of waiting out a year and coming with an ex-class or...
And I went to Wilmington College which is in Wilmington, Ohio, which is another Quaker School.
And then I transferred in my junior year.
So, and having been raised in the family and in the community that you've been telling us about, with a lot of awareness of issues, social issues and justice issues,
how were you of other things that were happening in the country?
I was part of the civil rights movement, but were you aware of earlier traditions and what was going on?
You know, were there particular movements for you?
Well, I mean, I was very aware of the Vietnam War and the lead up to that.
And I was one of the protesters at my at Irland, you know, that would go regularly to stand and witness against the war.
And it was, however, my friend Dorothy, we referred to as Landsberg. She was Dorothy Shelton, then.
I met her at Irland College and she was involved with...
Her father was an internal revenue lawyer in Washington, D.C.
And he helped get her a summer internship with John Dorr in the summer of 64, where she worked very hard on the developing an index of...
from the FBI reports on clan members, which was then subsequently helpful when jury selection took place in a number of cases.
And, well, I mean, we were all... she was part of the student at, you know, political activities committee and so was I.
And then I met John Dorr. That's when I met John Dorr.
She invited him to speak at a convocation at Irland. And she didn't want to introduce him because she was afraid he would tease her in front of everyone.
So she got her buddy Jean to introduce him. And while we were eating together, he said, well, have you ever thought about joining me establishment?
And I said, no. And he said, why don't you do that? And why don't you give it a try?
And so since I wasn't focused on where I would be after I graduated, we had thought about going to law school, but that wasn't really a passion.
So I did, in fact, apply and was accepted as a research analyst for the Civil Rights Division, the Department of Justice in June of 65.
And I then went on to qualify my loyalty oath and I still had the job.
And what did you say?
I've inserted in the loyalty oath that I would, in so far as my conscience would allow, I would, you know, support and defend the United States.
So they kept me anyway. And that was when I started working as a research analyst.
And went from, we worked on all kinds of cases. We worked on school desegregation cases, police brutality cases, employment cases, during the time that I was there. I left in the spring of 1970.
Okay. So let's switch over to John and then we'll bring him up to that same moment.
So John, if you could introduce yourself and a little bit about your background as well, it would be wonderful.
Well, I'm John Rosenberg. I'm Jean's husband of 46 years. She's put up with me over that period, hopefully for another maybe not 46, but a few more.
I was born in Germany October 7, 1931, a long time ago.

My father was a teacher in the Jewish school system. I say the Jewish school system. He actually, when Adolf Hitler set up a secret required the schools to be segregated so that Jewish kids were separated out.
He and another man set up the school for Jewish kids and the city was named Magdeburg, Germany.
The city then, about 300,000 people, a large industrial center, which was bombed pretty heavily during the war.
And I grew up there and then as a young child, the first day of school is a big deal.
And if I remember, you go to school, start around Easter and they give you a big conical bag with all kinds of gifts in it.
I say that because we have, there's a family picture of me holding this conical bag with our gifts on the first day of school.
But I was actually, by that point, my father's class. He was a teacher and then I also worked for the Jewish welfare agency.

He was teaching courses to people who were interested in going to Israel. He wanted to go to Israel, but my mother said, no, we are, if we leave the country, we're going to go to the United States because her mother and father and two sisters had immigrated to the country and she wasn't really interested in going to Israel.
Although he had had one sister and a brother go to Israel at an early time as into a small cooperative community.
In any event, we lived in a building next to a synagogue. We had an apartment there because dad worked for that welfare agency and he also assisted the rabbi and the local congregation.

We were there during Kristallnacht and they bombed the synagogue. They didn't, they were somewhat careful about the synagogue because there was a hospital next door. Otherwise, as you probably know, all the other synagogues, primarily were burned down or the ones most synagogues in Germany were burned.

The way my dad was in a concentration camp for 11 days and we were fortunate enough to get out of the country. We came, we spent a year in Holland. My father started a school in this detention camp and we had no materials but he taught by the Socratic Method teaching us verbally.

We came to this country on February 22, 1940 on pretty much the last ship. His brother and mother were on board. I'm not sure about his mother. I think both of another ship of several weeks lighter in the ship was required. It was turned around because the war started.

They didn't get out. His brother was killed. He lost the, there was another family. One of his sisters had married. The fellow was on a stock exchange who was, they were fairly well off. They had five kids in Rotterdam and that family was gone and family in Germany.

Most of his, so he lost his substantial and his mother was also killed during that period. She lived right very close to the end of the war. In fact, his brother were married during the, while he was in the concentration camp and we met his widow in Israel several years later.

But so we came and then we, my father when we came to this country, it was very difficult to find a job. There were so many immigrants in New York. And so he learned of community in Spartanburg, South Carolina and Gastonian North Carolina. They had substantial Jewish communities but no rabbi.

And so he, that's what drew him there. I mean, we always, I'm not sure which comes first. But during the week he swept floors in a textile mill and on weekends he alternated going to these two communities. And I always thought it was pretty amazing because he was writing sermons when he could hardly just still learning to speak English.
But after three years we then moved. He learned of a better position in a textile mill in Gastonia or in Lowell North Carolina.
Fellow who came from our hometown who in Magdeburg, a parent who had it mill they are brought a bunch of equipment and started textile mill.

And the idea learned went to work as an office manager if I remember right. But he basically learned the textile business and stayed as a white collar worker and was very active in the local synagogue and I grew up in town of Gastonia for practical purposes. We'd been in Spartanburg for three years.
So I grew up in Gastonia and you know worked after school and scouting was a big part of my life. I was an eagle scout and spent summers as a counselor and went off to Duke University as a freshman.
May I ask what the racial dynamics were in Gastonia at that time?
I would first tell about the fact that being a German immigrant they took away the binoculars. I just think that's an interesting caveat.

After the war started when we were aliens, my parents weren't citizens and Gene always said the FBI or Treasury agents came to our apartment one day knocked on the door and they required they took the binoculars that my parents had and the shortwave, the radio that had a capability of getting shortwave.

But they were quite embarrassed to see that we had Franklin Delano Roosevelt's picture on the wall and you have this fairly patriotic thankful family to be in this country and they came and took this equipment away from us.
It was I think mutually embarrassed very. They eventually brought it back after the war. We got this stuff back.
But we were Gastonia was a segregated society. We had black schools. We had you were living in a segregated society.
You always think back on those days the Jewish community, most of the members of that community as was true and many southern communities were pretty well off.
They had their parents and grandparents had come to these communities or come to the area of his petalers and started selling merchandise and then eventually built a retail store.
The community was well off. I'm not sure that anybody had ever seen a portrait of what we were relatively poor Jewish working family until we got there.
It was a nice community and several other people were. I think they respected my father a lot because he had such a huge knowledge of Jewish culture and could read Hebrew.

We could quote the next verse in the Torah for five books of Moses if you started him anywhere in there which was not that unusual for someone who had all that biblical background or studied in the some he had been to the seminary.
He actually came from family with nine children in northwest Germany and one of his early teaching assignments was in my mother's hometown.
She was a young 16 year old and they got to know each other and by when she was 18 they got married.
He was working in the mill and I had some of jobs in the mill and it was as you lived as you know, every Jewish community was thankful to be there economically.
They couldn't get into the country club for many despite their wealth. Most of them were not at that point. The country clubs were segregated.
But they certainly took no major initiatives in trying to help integrate the community or call attention to the fact that this is wrong.
I think you basically grew up with it and when you move into that system looking back, I'm sure my parents were so grateful to be there that they were. That was the last thing they had in mind.
Although my parents, my mother especially, I mean my summer job, I worked off when you're in the mill you interact with lots of black folks.
But everybody kind of knows where the lines are.

I don't know if there was any Gastonia's history is sort of interesting. There are book called preachers and mill hands. They had riots in the 20s and 30s around the textile worker issue and working conditions.
But it wasn't racial. It wasn't that was not a racial issue.
So the schools were totally segregated and that's just the way life was. My mother had my mom was known to be a very friendly person. She was and didn't start work.
She decided after the kids were all gone that she wanted to be a saleswoman to do work in the local department store which she did.
But she had people coming by where they were black or white and she would interact with them and offer them bite the eat or whatever.
And I think the family in that sense, I didn't have don't recall. I don't think I had any friends in the black community.

A few years ago, not too many years ago, 10 years ago I think Gastonia, the class president of the year before me or two years, promoted a joint anniversary of the classes from 1948 so that the black and white graduates would now be adults would be together.

I think it was a one time thing. But I remember that Gaston County Gaston County is actually one of the voting rights cases. I went to the Supreme Court. I think they challenged the mechanism under which Gaston County was selected.
But from that I was a Duke in a, I went to undergraduate school at Duke. It wasn't, it was sort of between Duke and the University of North Carolina.

And there was a trustee at the time named C Park who was from Gastonia. And he encouraged me to go to Duke because he thought he could secure and he did secure a tuition scholarship for me for the first four, what we hope would be four years.

Unfortunately, he died after the first year. And I was then interviewed and said, you're making too much money working in the dining room where you got chips for food or whatever. And they said, you got to have to pay your own tuition.
Which I did and stayed in, but it was always sort of gosh. I was pretty poor. My dad was sending me 25 bucks a month or something and they took away the tuition scholarship.
But I did get, I was a chem major Duke and I in ROTC. And so I ended, we went into the United States Air Force. And I spent three years in England as an navigator.
London, very close to a lot of my colleagues had a very interesting year fly. We went, at that time it was a period of the Cold War.
So many of our exercises were on the close to the Russian border. We dropped the special forces and a very military period.

Totally contrary to what I was picketing the Pentagon. Right. Well, she was picketing the Pentagon. So after I came out of the service, I worked for a year for a chemical firm, Roman Hassan, Philadelphia.

So when the next court looked at it, they would say, he, this is wrong.
Right.
And you're going to reverse that judge.
So you had a sense of the long haul of them.
Well, you wanted it to come around to what you were thinking.
It was going to be a long haul.
And I think in that way, the, if you look at the history of the Voting Rights Act and the testimony from Kat,
I guess Katzmacken, these other people who testified, they basically had said, this is not going to work in just one case at a time.
We are, we are doing the best we can through the courts.
But blacks are entitled to vote and we need to have it happen.
Right.
And so Martin Luther King that marched all together.
And so I think the, the cases had a real place.
I mean, it was like the first attack.
It was the first law.
It was a launch.
And, and I think the division in the department, the attorney general, they deserve great credit for that.
It's not a story everybody knows.
I mean, it didn't, the movement, I mean, the relationship might have been better, but Bob Moses knew that he could call John any hour.

He's been become an educator and we've seen him a couple of times since then.
And Lawrence Giot, you know, you know, you know, some of these names and many of them we knew and saw very often.
But I think you could see that, I mean, that was enough to keep, I mean, if you knew Fannie Lou Hamer or Mr. Eskridge,
and she's out there chopping cotton or thinking she might lose her job or Eskridge is set all out there by himself,
he wants just all he wants is to get his kid into school, which certainly is not the most popular thing to do.
I mean, he's really, they're putting their lives online.
I don't think, I never felt like we were putting our life on the line.
I mean, I think you've got that little yellow, you've got that black thing in your pocket.
This is one, I would just happen to see it, it was the one that has me up front.
But you know, you've got that little black thing in your pocket is the Justice Department ID, which people I think knew.
I mean, most of these rural counties, they figured you were either an insurance salesman or you were a Justice Department lawyer.
It goes, you're in a suit and tie.
Yeah, I mean...
But some of the civilized workers dressed well, too, at least, at certain point.
Yeah, I don't think they were too conscious, but they certainly didn't look.
I mean, they were students from all over.

Black section is known as Balance Do.

Like going to the...

All of it, a few days later, they let some of the really older people out.
The guy's name was Artie Burr, but we filed this case, and John put me on the stand to talk about,
I sat there and watched them convict all these people, this justice of the peace.
And so we filed that case, and actually eventually we...
That was for Judge Clayton and we lost.
What happened in the meantime was the National Council of Church has got some bond money, and got these people out.
But I went up there every day to that penal farm to interview these people for about two weeks.
But they were sitting there in terrible shape.
For no reason at all.
I mean, it's kind of like these things you see in other...
Nobody was killed in that situation, but it was...
It would never happen if you were white, if that was a white group, never in your life at that time.

And the people who drafted that.

Unless you took off to be part of that.

Resurrection City?

The big... because John Mitchell...
Well, no, there were...

Trying to ensure that that was going to be a safe process.
During the riots...
What's after King's assassination?

Now there...

and he and I went to Memphis, and we prepared the extradition papers to get Ray, James R. Ray,
back to this country from England.

You all just came from Memphis.
We were just there, we just went to the museum.
The New... so right's museum?
Yeah.

Really?

Oh, really?
I mean, this was towards the very beginning,
so we were getting affidavits from police officers and fingerprint people.
I'm looking at the gun and what we needed was enough information together to get him extradited back to this country.
I think the extradition papers were really prepared in Washington by some...
but he and I went down and we got all this information together.
And then built to you in the Civil Rights Division?
Interesting.

but they wanted us to do that.
They wanted me and I don't know where we were at the time.
Maybe in the Carroll County?

And then after that, I remember when these rights in Washington, Cyrus Vance, was my boss.
