And I spent a lot of time with him for a couple of days trying to...
they were arresting a lot of people, you know, and they were sending him to a place in...
Marlund, Quantico?
We were quite a little vague, but for me, some of the division was very much involved in that again.
I think again, partly because the protesters and the black activists knew that they were going to get a fair shake.
People were going to be respectful and appreciate what they did, but that we had a job to do.
And Jean mentioned the poll tax case a while ago.
I just don't know how much you want to say about that, but the...
you know, the poll tax was another one of those reconstruction act things that required people to pay, cheer, to be able to...
as a condition of voting.
And then it was cumulative over time and was only throughout the South.
It was another device to prevent people from registering to vote.
So the legislation that implemented the Voting Rights Act, there had been one case that had been...

I think one of the Lawyers Committee might have filed a case, but anyway, the Voting Rights Act of 1965 required the Attorney General to forthwith file an action to contest the poll tax in the Southern States.
And we had those cases ready to go, and on the Monday, I went down on the Monday after the act was signed.
I went to miss to Jackson and filed the poll tax case in Mississippi.
Turned out, I ended up working and preparing the Alabama poll tax case.
And Steve Pollock, who was by then the Assistant Attorney General, was maybe his first assistant still before he became Assistant Attorney General.
I think so. He argued the case.
So we put all these cases together and we basically had this history of segregation, all the segregation statutes.
The interesting thing in that Alabama case was that...
and this is in the opinion that the court issued, just before, even after we filed this case, there was an election coming up locally, a statewide election.
And Governor Wallace had a brochure printed with his picture on it that went...
that the highway patrol went all over Alabama, distributing it to all the white schools to take home to their parents.
Not only to the white schools. I mean, this is done after we filed this case.
So we put it in the record. And if you open the opinion, you see this brochure with Wallace's picture on it describing this thing.
Even down to the right before trial, they were going to do everything they could to prevent blacks or at least to maximize the white election and electorate.
And so there was all this history. We had this legislative history of all these terrible remarks made by the legislators way back in 1900.
The only reason we need to stop these blacks from voting.
I mean, it's so... there's no hidden language. I mean, it's very emphatic.
And then, all the way forward, describing the statistics. But then the court invalidated the poll tax in all of these states in two or three different cases.
But it was another... and it was, again, the result of all this litigation that had come along. I mean, where we had all this information and that had come.
But it was another big... it was a nice case that has spent a good bit of time on. And it had been a case before Judge Johnson, Fred Gray, who is a prominent black attorney and Montgomery still.
If you have an... he's really a terrific person. He filed a case contesting one of the elections right after...

which, I think, was really a local election. And I represented the government that Judge... what Judge Johnson started to do was in most of these civil rights cases, whether the government brought it or not, he would order the government to...
the United States to be made a party because he wanted the civil rights division lawyers in his court.
John or one of his representatives. So, I... this was not a case that had a particularly good outcome, but I sat there with Fred Gray probably for...
I think this case took two or three weeks of putting on testimony about the election and the getting all the statistics in.
And we were advising the court this best we could about what to do. And in the end, if I'm... in the end, I think all that came out of it really was...
maybe some reforms in the way they were supposed... they would needed to carry out their election process.
But the election itself, if the officials was not overturned, but it was Fred's in that case that he had brought, but got to know him pretty well.
But Judge Johnson was really... you talk about a hero, you asked about the...
And so, so we're all... all of these judges, Judge Wisdom and Judge Brown and Judge Bell, who was from Atlanta, eventually went to the 11th Circuit.
He was the Attorney General. He was trying to think who the other Circuit Judge judges were.
That were on that 5th Circuit.
I just can't remember all their names, but it's... sure was a blessing to have them on the court at the time, because so many of these district judges were southerners who were...

who made it very difficult to litigate in front of them, and it took so much longer, or the same thing in Louisiana, except for Judge Johnson, same thing in Northern Alabama in the Birmingham District, because you had these...
a Northern and Southern Arneastern and Western District Federal Court in each one of these states, as we have today.
And so, putting these initial trial records together was invariably for the next court, and it was a slow process.
But it really, in the end, it really made the record for the Voting Rights Act. I mean, it's quite a story, and that sense, Brian tells that history in his book really well, I think.
Did you have a permanent residence during these days, or were you... I mean, it sounds like you're in the field most of the time.
We lived in Washington. When we went to Houston, we moved to Houston for six months, because that was going to be a fairly large case.
But in the 60s, even before Gene came, we were probably gone. John described, it's interesting in this Florida law of you note that he wrote some years ago.
He said, he'd go outside on a Friday. You could see the briefcase was lined up outside the Civil Rights Division offices.
And back then, people would get on the plane. They'd either go to Memphis or Birmingham or wherever they were going.
And the airplane were the DC-3s. Remember, there were C-46s in the Air Force, or C-47s, I guess.
And they'd take off on a Friday afternoon for two weeks, and we'd go down and usually photograph several counties,
we would have made the arrangements to get the FBI in, and you try to photograph four or five counties.
I also did the Jackson Heins County, that's Jackson, with photographed all those records.
And I know they remember, they had a courthouse in Rankin, I think it's a name of the town, not only in Jackson, but you're looking at thousands and thousands of records.
And then in some of them, in some of the larger cities like Jackson, some blacks had gotten registered to vote.
But again, it was not carte blanche. If you knew you, or they'd have to register some, and get a little more sophisticated, then it would in the rural counties.
But we would all have responsibilities for several of these voting cases at once, and some of them, as they came to trial,
the first John tried many of these cases himself, and Bob Owen tried the case, and eventually those of us, the line lawyers, grew up a little bit under this system.

It's a very disciplined system, and in the government, you write a complaint, and you send it up to the next person who redlines it, and sends it back to you, and you gradually is, I mean, and the, yeah.

So we learned to be pretty good lawyers under that system. I mean, I always tell people, so I think it stood me really in good stead when we came here, because I had to teach one to teach another group of lawyers how to deal with that.
And we'll probably just take another 10 minutes or so.
So I went close to the final.
Oh, no, I was just thinking about when he said they really over-prepared every case, and visualized the Houston school system.
We took, and color-coded, on maps of Houston, every single residence in the enrolled student, to show how the lines were, it was just, you just can't not imagine how labor intensive.
That was before computers. I mean, it was just amazing.

We would call them dot-maps. You know, blacks had, we're red, and whites were green, and we showed where the schools were, and they were busing black student packs, white schools, and white schools pass black schools.
Houston was, and that case had gone on for many years, and Judge Connolly, we had four or five lawyers working with me, plus how many research analysts?
It was kind of like an antitrust case, foot lockers of records, and the Houston school case going way back, and what we wanted was busing to try to remedy the effects of this panel.
The effects of this past discrimination, and try to get some reasonable integration into the Houston school system, because the inner city black schools were pretty bad.
They spent all their money out in suburbia. The problem, one of the problems was you had this black core, and then you had the beginnings of a donut around it, of Spanish speakers.

And so some of the parent, and then you had white suburbia. And so how do you really fix that? Judge Connolly in the end paired up a lot of schools. Many of them were black and mixed Spanish speakers, Chicano.
But then they appealed that, I think in the end, the court of appeals, we were gone by then, required some busing.
But I think the best thing that happened was that the voters threw the school board out, and they threw out this lawyer on the other side, and that had happened in many places.

This lawyer became a millionaire off the school case. I mean, he was a corporate lawyer, but they'd been resisting desegri in his school system for years, and this lawyer had made a living off that really for many years.

And the same thing in Little Rock, I had a couple of cases in Arkansas. This big firm in downtown Little Rock, representing these school districts, these rural school districts that were still totally segregated.
And they were, I mean, they did other work, but they were well paid to keep the system segregated. And so in Houston, they finally ditched him, and they had a whole new school board.
And I think that over time, Houston, I mean, it's an enormous system. Now it's twice as big as it was when we were there.
But I think they've really reformed them on their schools. But those are different. Same thing with employment cases. I mean, we haven't mentioned them.
When we started when I became chief of the Western section, we sued the electrical workers in Las Vegas, because the union was, blacks couldn't get a job.
You couldn't get into the apprenticeship, couldn't get into the union because you were black, which you hardly ever realized. And it's still pretty much a segregated town.
I mean, the housing patterns, we sued the steam fitters in Los Angeles, sued the roadway express. That was James Case, roadway trucking company with my, well, they were in Birmingham, right?
Yeah, they were in Birmingham.

Yeah, but the division, the same thing with schools afterwards. There were a lot of schools in Indianapolis, schools in Oklahoma City as the, as the after the Civil Rights Act of 64, the division really expanded its work into other parts of the country as well.

I mean, it was not on, it needed to happen because the all segregation didn't just happen in the South. It might have been more de facto in some areas in the larger, much more difficult to do the Chicago school case or Indianapolis.

So let's use this as a segue then into your move out of out of the division and to hear and the work that you took on here and this idea of taking on other social justice issues and how these maybe related one to the other.

Well, I mean, I think they are, as they are related. I mean, we hoped we could, I guess I was hoping that I could continue doing public interest work. I did talk to some private firms, but we went on this big camping trip and wasn't quite sure.
But we left the division because the administration changed and the cases that got filed, what congressional district they were in seemed to become more important than what was in the case.

Well, and they retreated on school, these are the issues. I mean, I think it not an easy decision and there was some civil rights division lawyer revolts. I don't know if you remember, there was some of that history when they felt that the division wasn't doing enforcing the law the way they should.

And actually that took most of the half of my way were in Houston. So, but whether it belonged to, if you are going to leave, especially if you're in a position of some responsibility, whether they're going to fill your shoes with somebody who is not going to do the kind of work at the level that you were doing it.
People who are really committed should you stay and fight it out and try to push him to do that or can you.

But we decided also because I think if you stay in government service, you sort of start pricing yourself out of the mark. I mean, you're making fairly good money. You're doing fairly good economically too.
And if you're going to switch, of course we took a big die, you know, we took our money. It's only half.
Well, we came here, but we do, you know, we decided money wasn't all that important to us.
Were you tired too? Did you need a break from that taste or not?
Well, we went on this camping trip for three months with a little baby. Went to Canada and went to all the national parks.
I mean, we sort of left in the beginning of the summer and decided then we formally didn't leave, I think, when we came back.
But we went on a put up a tent or learned how to put up a tent in New Jersey, which we bought at a Morse end store.
And then we went up to the state, up the coast, up the New End, up to Canada.
We took probably a couple of months and our Michael was what, three, four, he was six months old.
Jean was breastfeeding. And we just got away from it. And right after we left one of the firms, one we come back for an interview.
And I said, no, we didn't want to do that. But along the way, one of our colleagues, Terry Lensner, who is,
a book is going to come out, who also was at that point, the director of legal services for the Office of Economic Opportunity for OEO.
And they had started some legal services programs with OEO money. There wasn't a lot of that going.
Most of those programs were in the Northeast where, at that time, under the Office of Economic Opportunity Act, you could only get a project if the governor agreed.
You had to stay, you had to stay, you had to stay okay for whatever happened. And so there were a few legal service programs outside of the Northeast, California.

So this, Terry had been approached by some lawyers in Charleston who once started public interest law and was public interest law firm focused on the coal issues, symptomatic issues of poverty and the coal fields.
And so he got in touch with us and said, why don't you go down and take a look at this. I would like, I'm willing to put a little money in starting supporting this kind of work in Appalachia.
And so we came down, we stopped and visited with folks in Charleston, and then we came down here, camped out here at General Iowa State Park, put the tent up.
Jean said you better see if there any bears around. Foxes, they're wanting somebody out there but us. I mean, it was at the end of the season.
I figured if there were animals that were eating off the campers and there were no more campers.
So we did, we were actually on our way to Florida. It was high holidays. Jewish high where we were going to go.
To my parents who had retired to Florida. So we came down here and we interviewed Harry, Harry Coddle and White Spurg who wrote, night comes to the Comberlands and was on.
He was on the Apple Red Board and was Virginia that already started a little corporation up there.
And just for the record Apple Red stands for Apple Action Research and Defense Fund.
That was the original name and several years later when we had to split the programs because of political problems.
I mean, we weren't welcomed down here anyway. So we came here and we, the Preston's Burg, we came to Preston's Burg.
We came to Charleston and talked to the folks who were there that Terry had talked with.
And then there were, there was some lawyers who were unlicensed who were already here and then fell on him to Howard Thorkelson.
And Howard was going to leave and they asked us to, he was going to be the deputy for Westford for Kentucky.

Anyway, so he was leaving. So we came to Preston's Burg and then drove down to White Spurg to talk to Coddle and said, we'd think about it and drove and drove on down to see my parents and the real thought was there, there are some real environmental issues, there are some poverty issues.
There are certainly a lot of poverty and there are very few lawyers who are going to, who appear to be available to challenge these.

Most of these private attorneys were tied up by co-companies and the issues that were described to us by Harry, this broad formed deed and the environmental issues around strip mining and coal mine health and safety, Black Long.

And the poverty issues consumer law and there were a lot of, at that time there were a lot of basic issues around food stamps and Medicaid and the early parts of that program applicants were given a very, had a difficult time.
Local time, just local welfare offices were very, very negative and were not very, they weren't working with the NIGG ARDO.
Anyway, it was just, how could your dad spelling?
Oh no.
They were just terrible, I mean, they, they, they, you would think they were treating these applicants so badly and people were having a difficult time getting food stamps.
Very, very much the same.
Food family were.

Who you got and jobs were all very political because the coal that only two industry, the largest employer invariably in these counties was the co-companies and the second largest were the school districts.
And they were very politicized and there was much of a fiefdom as we saw in the white power structure in the, in the south.
But I think, you know, what, what was your area that you covered?
Well, we started, I mean, technically the organization was for the Appalachian counties in Kentucky, of which there were 42.
We subsequently gave, gave away, we gave moved six counties into another legal services programs, the ones around Ashland.
But the program covered these 37 counties and so when, when I first came, we had one or two, we had an office on the campus University of Kentucky when in Barbaraville and here.
We eventually, when the Legal Services Corporation Act was passed and finally there was federal funding.
There was a lot of controversy about the legislation to create a structure for free legal services programs, a lot of opposition.
When we came to here, there was a lot of opposition from the local bar because they thought we were taking money away from them.
And the community because they thought we were communist?
Well, that too.

Because our predecessors, they had a group of, a group of, Apple, call of activist outside agitators, call, they were Appalachian volunteers who had come here to volunteer in various poverty programs.
And they too lived with local families and encouraged them to apply for benefits and health care.
And hospitals at that time were turning away people didn't have any money.
They just told me we don't do it.
Unwent, I mean pregnant moms couldn't get a delivery.
A lot of terrible stories.
The situation was really pretty grim.
So, and these...
A little while to work your way into the...
Well, I mean, there was a case of when Supreme Court involving the Appalachian volunteers, where I think one of the volunteers was...
Volunteers had threatened a local official or might have even slugged him.
I can't map them.
No, he slugged them.
Well, I don't know. Clark, did he hit them?
I can't say that.
There was a lot of controversy and they...
And that, there was outside agitators and spades.
And they were trying to encourage people to apply for benefits and to get...
And that sort of thing.
And there was...
Health care was very...
It was very poor.
I mean, there were one of our friends started eventually, started a free clinic out here on Mud Creek.
But there were all those issues that...
If you're trying to promote social justice or environmental justice, but...
And make the system operate a little more fairly, so that poor people have a chance.
And then it isn't just who you are.
And that you have...
So often, at that level, a lawyer makes a big difference.
Many of these people have legal issues.
Family problems.
Disability problems.
I mean, disability benefits.
Consumer law, you can't...
How can you deal with it for closure if you don't have a lawyer?
So your play, I'm sure, in the 43 years has been full all the time.
I mean, especially covering such a wide area.
Absolutely.
Do you have a full play?
You've been there.
So when we were 10 off, we expanded to 10 off...
11 offices.
Okay.
And this was our main administrative office.
And then we started back when I retired in 2002.
We said we were fortunate we started a separate nonprofit to do primarily focus on the coal issues.
Call the Appalachian Citizens Law Center.
But over that time, when we expanded, each of these offices
would cover several counties.
We were hoping we would have four lawyers, at least in every office.
So we could do the day-to-day work and do a major, a larger case.
We sued the state...
Like we sued the state park system.
Because they were discriminating against blacks.
Still, couldn't get a job in the state park system.
If you were black, primarily you'd end up at the bottom.
That was familiar.
But that takes a fair amount of resources.
And locally and a local, a little office.
We did a lot of those fairly major cases.
And day-to-day divorces for people are custody matters that all of them take time.
And no private attorney is going to do them if they're not going to pay you.
So we have...
What I hope to do is have a first-class law firm for poor people away.
John Doerr wanted to put a good bunch of lawyers together for...
to represent the government.
So I think we did some of that.
I think we had a wonderful terrific group of lawyers who were in fairly remote locations in small towns in eastern Kentucky.
So you saw that as something you could do to train that next generation, the next cadre?
I hope there was a good goal.
I think that's what I wanted to do.
And hopefully these folks when you are in the community.
One of the big differences I do think is that I learned to appreciate was that we were in the south
and spent a lot of time preparing cases, but we went home to Washington.
When you're here and folks are in their communities, they also become part of the local community.
So if our kids are in these schools, we care about these schools.
And so Jean saw in a statewide advocacy school.
Whether we've been working on education for many years, not just us, but what you want.
You're trying to create a better community for everybody.
And whether it's schools or the environment.
And the environment is hard because coal miners want...
That's their jobs.
And when people start complaining that regulations are preventing them from mining coal,
it makes it hard to try to demonstrate that you need regulations to protect the environment.
And that that can be done.
You can mine coal responsibly and comply with regulations at the same time.
